prd.name: Root Discovery and Short References
prd.overview: Let users run prd commands from anywhere inside a repository and address PRD files by short references instead of full paths. The tool finds the PRD root by walking up the directory tree for a marker, the same way git locates a repository. Once a root is known, short references like `comments/likes-saves` resolve to the matching file under the discovered PRD directory.

requirement.name: Root discovery
requirement.description: The tool finds the PRD root by walking upward from the current working directory and stopping at the first ancestor that carries a marker.
requirement.rule: Discovery starts at the current working directory and walks up through parent directories until a marker is found or the filesystem root is reached.
A `.prd-tool.toml` file at any ancestor directory marks that directory as the PRD root.
An ancestor directory containing `prd/index.xml` is recognized as the PRD root even without a configuration file.
When both markers exist in the same ancestor, the `.prd-tool.toml` marker takes precedence.

requirement.name: Short references
requirement.description: Every command that takes a PRD file accepts either a path or a short reference of the form `module/feature`. Existing path-based invocations keep working.
requirement.rule: An argument that resolves to an existing file is used as-is, regardless of whether a PRD root is present.
A reference of the form `module/feature` resolves to the matching PRD file under the discovered PRD directory.
References ending in `.xml` resolve the same way as references without the suffix.
When a reference cannot be resolved, the tool exits with an error that names both markers it searched for and the directory where the search started.

requirement.name: Discovery commands
requirement.description: Commands that surface the discovered root and the PRDs it contains, plus a no-argument default for stats.
requirement.rule: `prd root` prints the resolved repository root, the resolved PRD directory, and the marker source that was matched.
When no root can be found, `prd root` exits non-zero and reports the markers it looked for and the directory where it started.
`prd ls` lists every PRD reference under the discovered PRD directory, one per line, suitable for piping; the index file itself is excluded from the listing.
`prd ls module` lists only the references that live under the given module directory.
`prd stats` invoked with no argument operates on the index file under the discovered PRD directory.

`prd ls --unfinished` (also accepted as `-u`) lists only PRD references that still have unfinished work — at least one rule whose status is not ✅, or at least one bug whose status is not Fixed. References whose rules are all done and have no open or fix-pending bugs are omitted. The flag composes with the optional module argument.

`prd stats --unfinished` (also accepted as `-u`) restricts the report to features that still have unfinished rules or non-Fixed bugs; fully-complete features are omitted from the per-feature rows while the rollup totals continue to reflect every file scanned.

requirement.name: Configuration
requirement.description: The `.prd-tool.toml` file is optional and may override the location of the PRD directory.
requirement.rule: Setting `dir` under the `[prd]` section in `.prd-tool.toml` places the PRD directory at that path, resolved relative to the toml file's directory.
When `.prd-tool.toml` cannot be parsed, the tool falls back to the default PRD directory rather than failing.
Unknown keys or sections in `.prd-tool.toml` are ignored so the schema can grow without breaking older files.
